bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — feature-based folder structure, colocation, barrel exports
bytes.title: Vue Feature-Based Folder Structure — Colocation & Barrel Exports Done Right
bytes.body:
Grouping Vue files by type (`/components`, `/composables`, `/stores`) breaks down fast once your app grows. Feature-based structure colocates everything a domain owns: its components, composables, Pinia stores, and route definitions live together under `/features/auth/`, `/features/dashboard/`, etc.

Each feature folder gets an `index.ts` barrel that controls its public API. Only what's exported from `index.ts` is fair game for other features — internals stay private by convention. This prevents cross-feature spaghetti imports like `../../features/auth/components/LoginForm.vue`.

Vue Router's lazy-loading pairs naturally here: `component: () => import('@/features/dashboard/pages/DashboardPage.vue')` keeps each feature's bundle chunk self-contained. Pinia stores defined inside the feature folder stay scoped — no global store sprawl. Composables like `useAuth()` exported from the barrel mean consumers never care about the internal file layout. When a feature grows too large, you split it into sub-features without touching any external import paths — the barrel absorbs the restructure.

bytes.topic: Component Design — single responsibility, 150-line rule, presentation vs logic separation
bytes.title: Vue Component Design — Single Responsibility, the 150-Line Rule, and Splitting Logic from Templates
bytes.body:
A Vue component doing too much is easy to spot: a 400-line `<script setup>` block, template conditionals three levels deep, and composables that mutate DOM state. The 150-line rule is a forcing function — if your SFC exceeds it, you almost certainly have two components hiding in one.

Separate presentation from logic by extracting data-fetching and state into a composable, then passing the result down as props. A `UserCard.vue` should receive `user`, `isLoading`, and `onFollow` — it should never call `useFetch` itself. That call belongs in `useUserProfile.ts`, consumed by a container component like `UserProfileView.vue`.

Vue's `defineProps` and `defineEmits` make the contract explicit. A purely presentational component has only props in, emits out — no `inject`, no store reads, no side effects. This makes it trivially testable with `@vue/test-utils` `mount` and a props stub, and reusable across any parent that can satisfy the interface.

Watch for the red flags: `watch` inside a component that also renders a table, `computed` chains longer than 3 steps, or a template with more than one `v-if`/`v-else-if` chain. Each is a signal to extract.
bytes.code_snippet:
<!-- UserCard.vue — presentation only -->
<script setup lang="ts">
defineProps<{ user: User; isLoading: boolean }>()
defineEmits<{ follow: [userId: string] }>()
</script>

<!-- UserProfileView.vue — container -->
<script setup lang="ts">
import { useUserProfile } from '@/composables/useUserProfile'
import UserCard from './UserCard.vue'

const { user, isLoading, follow } = useUserProfile(route.params.id as string)
</script>

<template>
  <UserCard :user="user" :is-loading="isLoading" @follow="follow" />
</template>

bytes.topic: State Management — local vs lifted vs context vs external store, when to use each
bytes.title: Vue State Management — Local, Lifted, Provide/Inject, or Pinia?
bytes.body: Vue gives you four tiers of state, each with a clear use case. Local `ref()`/`reactive()` inside `<script setup>` is the default — if only one component needs it, keep it there. Lift to props/emits when a parent needs to coordinate two siblings; use `defineProps` and `defineEmits` to keep the contract explicit. Reach for `provide`/`inject` when you have deep component trees and prop-drilling becomes painful — pass a reactive `ref` via `provide` so descendants stay reactive without coupling to every ancestor. Pinia is the answer when state spans unrelated parts of the app, needs to persist across route changes, or requires devtools/time-travel debugging. The most common mistake is skipping directly to Pinia for everything. A form's dirty state is local. A selected tab shared between two siblings is lifted. A theme preference passed three levels deep is provided. User session data accessed across five unrelated views belongs in a Pinia store. Match the scope of the state to the scope of the solution.

bytes.topic: Performance — memoization (useMemo/useCallback/computed), when it helps vs hurts
bytes.title: Vue computed() — When Memoization Helps vs Hurts
bytes.body:
Vue's `computed()` memoizes derived state automatically — it caches the result and only recomputes when reactive dependencies change. This is Vue's primary memoization primitive, not a manual optimization like React's `useMemo`. The distinction matters: wrapping a simple property access in `computed()` adds overhead with zero benefit. It shines when the derivation is expensive — filtering a large array, transforming a dataset, or building a lookup map from reactive source data.

Vue has no `useCallback` equivalent because methods defined in `<script setup>` are stable references by default — they don't recreate on render. If you're passing a method as a prop to a child using `defineProps`, the reference stays stable without any wrapping.

Over-memoizing hurts: every `computed()` registers a watcher and allocates a cache slot. For trivial derivations (`fullName = firstName + ' ' + lastName`), the watcher overhead exceeds savings. Reserve `computed()` for derivations that are either expensive to calculate or shared across multiple template expressions — reading the same derived value twice in a template already benefits from a single `computed()` cache.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — server state vs client state, TanStack Query patterns
bytes.title: Vue + TanStack Query — Server State Doesn't Belong in Pinia
bytes.body:
Most Vue apps over-reach with Pinia, stuffing it with data that's really owned by the server. Server state has a different contract: it goes stale, needs background refetching, and must handle loading/error lifecycle. Client state (UI toggles, form drafts, selected tab) is yours to own — Pinia is perfect there.

`@tanstack/vue-query` handles server state with composables that integrate directly with Vue's reactivity. Pass a reactive ref as a query key and the query auto-refetches when it changes — no watcher needed. `useQuery` returns `data`, `isPending`, `isError` as computed refs, so templates stay declarative.

For mutations, `useMutation` gives you `mutate()` and `onSuccess` to invalidate stale queries via `queryClient.invalidateQueries`. This keeps your Pinia stores thin — only UI state lives there — and eliminates the manual fetch-store-display ceremony that plagues most Vue data layers. Cache TTL, deduplication, and background sync come for free.

bytes.topic: Forms — controlled vs uncontrolled, React Hook Form + Zod pattern
bytes.title: Vue Forms — Controlled vs Uncontrolled, and the VeeValidate + Zod Pattern
bytes.body:
Vue's `v-model` makes controlled forms the default — every input binds to a reactive `ref`, giving real-time access to form state. Uncontrolled forms use `useTemplateRef()` to read DOM values only on submit — useful for file inputs or third-party widgets that own their own state.

For production forms, Vue's ecosystem answer to React Hook Form + Zod is VeeValidate with `@vee-validate/zod`. The `toTypedSchema()` adapter converts a Zod schema into VeeValidate's validation format, preserving TypeScript inference end-to-end. `useForm()` returns `handleSubmit`, `defineField`, and `errors` — `defineField` produces a `[modelValue, attrs]` tuple you spread onto `<input>` elements, keeping templates declarative.

Unlike RHF's `register()`, `defineField` uses Vue's two-way binding model, generating both a reactive ref and the `onUpdate:modelValue` handler automatically. Validation runs on blur by default; set `validateOnModelUpdate: true` in `useForm` config for eager per-keystroke feedback. No render-prop wrappers, no Controller components — just reactive state wired directly to a Zod schema with full type inference on the `handleSubmit` callback.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — prop typing, discriminated unions for variants, no any
bytes.title: Vue 3 — Prop Typing with Discriminated Unions for Variant Components
bytes.body:
Vue 3's `defineProps` with TypeScript generics lets you enforce exhaustive prop contracts at compile time. For a Button component with multiple visual variants, a plain `type: String` prop silently accepts garbage values. Instead, use a discriminated union via `PropType<T>` or the type-only `defineProps<{}>` syntax to lock variants down.

With the script setup + TypeScript combo, you skip `PropType` entirely — just declare an inline type. For conditional slot behavior or computed classes that branch on variant, TypeScript narrows the union automatically inside `if` checks, eliminating `any` casts and runtime guards.

Avoid `defineProps({ variant: String })` — it gives you `string | undefined` with zero narrowing. The type-safe path is `defineProps<{ variant: 'primary' | 'danger' | 'ghost' }>()`, which Vue's compiler transforms into runtime validator metadata automatically. Pair this with `withDefaults` to set a fallback without losing the union constraint.
bytes.code_snippet:
<script setup lang="ts">
type Variant = 'primary' | 'danger' | 'ghost'

const props = withDefaults(
  defineProps<{
    variant: Variant
    disabled?: boolean
  }>(),
  { variant: 'primary', disabled: false }
)

const classes = computed(() => ({
  'btn-primary': props.variant === 'primary',
  'btn-danger':  props.variant === 'danger',
  'btn-ghost':   props.variant === 'ghost',
}))
</script>

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error boundaries per route, handle loading/error/empty states
bytes.title: Vue Error Boundaries — Per-Route Error, Loading, and Empty State Handling
bytes.body:
Vue 3 lacks React-style error boundaries natively, but `onErrorCaptured` in a wrapper component gives you the same control per route. Define a `RouteShell.vue` that each route's layout uses — it captures descendant errors, exposes a loading slot driven by the router's navigation guard, and renders an empty state when data resolves to null or an empty array.

Use `defineAsyncComponent` with its built-in `loadingComponent` and `errorComponent` options for code-split route chunks — this handles the async import lifecycle without custom logic. For data-fetching errors, pair `onErrorCaptured` (returning `false` to stop propagation) with a reactive `error` ref. Pass `suspense: true` on async components to coordinate with `<Suspense>` boundaries, letting you co-locate the fallback UI with the feature rather than scattering try/catch across composables.

Empty state is the easiest to miss: always check `data.value?.length === 0` separately from `data.value === null` — the first means the fetch succeeded but returned nothing; the second means it hasn't resolved yet.

bytes.topic: Testing — test behaviour not implementation, Testing Library philosophy
bytes.title: Vue Testing Library — Test What Users See, Not What Vue Knows
bytes.body:
Vue Test Utils makes it tempting to assert on internal state: `wrapper.vm.isLoading`, `wrapper.vm.handleSubmit()`, or `wrapper.vm.$emit`. These tests break on safe refactors—rename a ref, extract a composable, and your suite turns red despite zero user-visible change.

@testing-library/vue flips the model. Query the DOM the way a user navigates it: `getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })`, `getByLabelText('Email')`, `findByText('Success')`. Fire events with `userEvent.click()` rather than calling component methods directly.

This matters most with composables. If `useAuth` internally swaps from a `ref` to a `reactive` object, a behaviour-first test doesn't care—it just checks whether the login button appears after a successful call. Implementation tests would shatter.

The rule: if your test imports the component's internal symbols or reads `wrapper.vm.*`, it's testing implementation. If it only reads the rendered output and fires DOM events, it's testing behaviour. Only the latter survives real-world iteration.
bytes.code_snippet:
import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/vue'
import userEvent from '@testing-library/user-event'
import LoginForm from './LoginForm.vue'

test('shows welcome message after successful login', async () => {
  render(LoginForm)

  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText('Email'), 'dev@example.com')
  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText('Password'), 'secret123')
  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /log in/i }))

  expect(await screen.findByText('Welcome back!')).toBeInTheDocument()
})

bytes.topic: Security — dangerouslySetInnerHTML, token storage, input sanitization
bytes.title: Vue Security — v-html, Token Storage, and Input Sanitization
bytes.body: Vue's `v-html` directive is the equivalent of React's `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` — it bypasses Vue's template compiler and injects raw HTML directly into the DOM. Never pass user-controlled data through it without sanitization. Use DOMPurify before binding: `v-html="sanitize(userContent)"` where `sanitize` wraps `DOMPurify.sanitize()`. For token storage, avoid `localStorage` for JWTs — it's accessible to any script on the page, making XSS attacks trivially extractable. Prefer `httpOnly` cookies managed server-side. If you must use client storage, scope tokens to `sessionStorage` and pair with a short expiry. For input sanitization in forms, Vue's `v-model` does not sanitize — it binds raw strings. Validate and strip on submission using a schema library like Zod or Valibot before sending to your API. Composables are a clean pattern here: a `useSanitizedForm()` composable can wrap reactive form state, run Zod `.parse()` on submit, and expose only safe values to the template layer. Defense-in-depth means sanitizing at the component boundary, not just the backend.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — ESLint react-hooks plugin, naming conventions, 50-line rule
bytes.title: Vue Code Quality — eslint-plugin-vue, Naming Conventions, and the 50-Line Composable Rule
bytes.body:
Vue's official linter, `eslint-plugin-vue`, enforces patterns the core team considers non-negotiable. Use the `vue3-recommended` ruleset as your baseline — it catches multi-root template misuse, v-bind shorthand drift, and unordered component options. Beyond the preset, add `vue/component-name-in-template-casing: ['error', 'PascalCase']` to ban `<my-button>` in favor of `<MyButton>`, and `vue/prop-name-casing: ['error', 'camelCase']` to keep prop declarations consistent with JavaScript conventions while templates use kebab-case automatically.

For composables, enforce the `use` prefix with `vue/custom-event-name-casing` and a local ESLint rule or team convention: every file in `composables/` must export a single function named `useX`. Keep that function under 50 lines — if `useUserProfile` grows past that, it's doing two things. Split it into `useUserData` and `useUserPermissions`. This boundary forces composables to stay single-responsibility and makes tree-shaking more predictable. Pair with `vue/define-macros-order` to standardize `defineProps` → `defineEmits` → `defineExpose` ordering in `<script setup>` blocks across your entire codebase.

bytes.topic: Accessibility — semantic HTML first, focus management, ARIA only when HTML falls short
bytes.title: Vue Accessibility — Semantic HTML First, ARIA Only When HTML Falls Short
bytes.body:
Vue's component model makes it easy to build `<div>`-heavy UIs that screen readers can't navigate. Start with semantic HTML: `<button>` for actions, `<nav>` for navigation, `<main>` for page content. Vue doesn't change this contract.

For focus management, use `ref` on elements and call `.focus()` after state transitions — e.g., move focus to a modal's first interactive element when `v-if` renders it. Use `nextTick` so the DOM is ready before focusing.

Avoid ARIA until native HTML genuinely can't express the pattern. A `<button>` doesn't need `role="button"`. A `<select>` doesn't need `aria-expanded`. Where ARIA is justified — custom comboboxes, live regions — bind attributes dynamically: `:aria-expanded="isOpen"`, `:aria-live="polite"`.

For route changes in Vue Router, announce navigation with a visually-hidden live region updated in the `afterEach` hook. Vue's reactivity makes this a single `ref` update. Scoped slots in UI library components (Headless UI, Radix Vue) handle most ARIA plumbing — prefer them over hand-rolling.

bytes.topic: Internationalization — never hardcode strings, react-i18next, Intl API for dates/numbers
bytes.title: Vue i18n Done Right — vue-i18n, Never Hardcoded Strings
bytes.body:
Hardcoded strings in Vue components are a maintenance trap. Use `vue-i18n` v9+ with the Composition API: call `useI18n()` to get `t()` and `locale` inside `<script setup>`. Define messages per locale in flat or nested JSON, then reference keys via `t('nav.home')` in templates — never raw strings.

For dates and numbers, skip moment.js entirely. Vue-i18n exposes `d()` and `n()` which delegate to the native `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and `Intl.NumberFormat` APIs. Pass a named format key configured in `datetimeFormats` or `numberFormats` on your `createI18n` instance. Locale switching is reactive — binding `locale.value = 'fr'` triggers re-renders across all consuming components automatically.

For async locale loading, lazy-load message files with `setLocaleMessage()` inside a `router.beforeEach` guard to avoid shipping all translations upfront. Always set `fallbackLocale` so missing keys degrade gracefully rather than rendering raw key paths.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP and how component patterns affect each
bytes.title: Vue Web Vitals — How Component Patterns Drive LCP, CLS, and INP
bytes.body: Vue's component model directly shapes all three Core Web Vitals. For LCP, wrapping above-the-fold content in `defineAsyncComponent` or `` delays the largest paint — keep hero images and primary headings in eagerly loaded components. Add `fetchpriority="high"` directly on the LCP ``; Vue doesn't do this automatically. For CLS, `v-if` is the primary culprit: it removes and reinserts DOM nodes, shifting surrounding content. Switch to `v-show` for toggled UI that exists on initial load, and add fixed-height skeleton placeholders before `v-for` lists arrive asynchronously — Vue reserves no space by default. For INP, Vue's scheduler batches reactive writes, but expensive `computed` properties called inside event handlers still run synchronously on the main thread. Use `shallowRef` for large arrays to bypass deep reactive traversal. Debounce `v-model` bindings on search inputs with `useDebounceFn` from VueUse. Avoid `watch({ deep: true, immediate: true })` on large objects — it blocks the frame before the browser can paint a response.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — cleanup in useEffect/onUnmounted/onDestroy, AbortController, removeEventListener
bytes.title: Vue Memory Leaks — Cleaning Up with onUnmounted and AbortController
bytes.body:
Vue's Composition API gives you onUnmounted to tear down side effects before a component leaves the DOM. Skip it and you'll leak event listeners, intervals, and pending fetch requests every time a component mounts. Every addEventListener needs a paired removeEventListener inside onUnmounted. Timers from setInterval must be cleared. Fetch calls need an AbortController whose signal you pass to fetch — abort it on unmount so stale responses don't update state on an already-destroyed component.

In the Options API, the equivalent hook is beforeDestroy (Vue 2) or beforeUnmount (Vue 3). The Composition API's onUnmounted fires after the component is torn down, so prefer beforeUnmount when you need the DOM still accessible.

watchEffect auto-tracks its dependencies and returns a stop handle, but if you register side effects inside it manually — event listeners, subscriptions — you still own cleanup via its implicit onCleanup callback argument. Vue won't do it for you. The pattern: register in setup, tear down in onUnmounted or onCleanup. No exceptions.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — singleton connection, reconnection logic, optimistic UI on send
bytes.title: Vue WebSockets — Singleton Connection, Reconnection, and Optimistic UI
bytes.body:
A singleton WebSocket in Vue lives at module scope, outside the composable function body — so multiple component instances share one connection rather than opening one per mount. Declare `let socket: WebSocket | null = null` at the top of `useChat.ts`; the composable checks `socket.readyState` before reconnecting.

Reconnection logic belongs in the `onclose` handler. A simple exponential backoff uses a module-level `reconnectDelay` that doubles on each failure and resets inside `onopen`. Clear any pending timeout in `onUnmounted` to prevent stale callbacks after the last consumer unmounts.

Optimistic UI means pushing outbound messages into the reactive `ref` array before `socket.send()` fires. If you need rollback on failure, track messages as `{ id, text, status: 'pending' | 'sent' | 'failed' }` and update `status` inside `onmessage`. Vue's fine-grained reactivity makes this clean — mutating `messages.value` triggers targeted re-renders without manual DOM work. For server-confirmed ordering, reconcile by message ID rather than array index to avoid flicker.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — never hardcode URLs or keys, validate env vars at startup with Zod
bytes.title: Vue Config Done Right — Validate Env Vars at Startup with Zod
bytes.body:
Vite exposes environment variables to Vue apps via `import.meta.env`, but only variables prefixed with `VITE_` are bundled into the client. Hardcoding API base URLs or keys directly in your composables or `main.ts` is a silent footgun — different environments, broken builds, no feedback until runtime.

Instead, create a `src/env.ts` module that parses and validates all required env vars using Zod at module load time. Import this module at the top of `main.ts` so the app fails fast with a descriptive error before mounting the Vue app tree. This prevents a misconfigured staging deploy from silently hitting production endpoints.

Expose the validated config as a plain typed object. Composables and services import from `env.ts`, never from `import.meta.env` directly. This single indirection gives you type safety, early failure, and a single place to audit every external dependency your Vue app touches. Pair with `.env.example` committed to the repo so new contributors know exactly which vars are required.

bytes.topic: API Layer — single HTTP client instance, interceptors for auth/retry, never raw fetch in components
bytes.title: vue API Layer — Single Axios Instance, Interceptors, No Raw fetch in Components
bytes.body:
Scattering `fetch()` calls across Vue components couples your UI to transport logic and makes auth changes a find-and-replace nightmare. Instead, create a single Axios instance in `src/api/client.ts` and export it as your app's only HTTP primitive.

Attach a request interceptor that reads the auth token from your Pinia store — not from `localStorage` directly — so the interceptor stays reactive to token refreshes. Add a response interceptor that catches 401s and either calls your refresh endpoint or redirects to login, then retries the original request with `error.config`.

Wrap domain calls in thin composables — `useUserApi()`, `usePostsApi()` — that import the shared client. Components call composables, composables call the client, the client talks to the network. Nothing else does. This makes mocking trivial in Vitest: replace the client import with `vi.mock('../api/client')` once, and every composable test gets a clean fake with zero component coupling. Interceptors also centralize logging, `AbortController` timeout policies, and base URL switching between environments.

bytes.topic: Animation — CSS for simple states, Framer Motion for complex, prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.title: Vue Animations — CSS Transitions, Framer Motion, and prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.body:
Vue's built-in `<Transition>` and `<TransitionGroup>` components handle simple state changes cleanly — enter/leave fades, list reorders, modal slides. For these, CSS is the right tool: zero JS overhead, GPU-composited transforms, and trivially respectable of user motion preferences via `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` in your transition classes.

For orchestrated sequences — staggered card reveals, drag-and-drop with spring physics, shared element transitions — reach for `@vueuse/motion` (Vue-native bindings over Motion's engine). It exposes a `v-motion` directive and `useMotion()` composable that integrate naturally with Vue's reactivity system.

Always gate complex animations: read `window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches` or use VueUse's `usePreferredReducedMotion()`. Pass the result into your motion variant to swap spring animations for instant state changes. Never rely solely on CSS media queries when JS controls your animation engine — the directive won't pick them up automatically.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — toast system at app root, modal management, confirmation dialogs as hooks
bytes.title: Vue Global UI Patterns — Toast, Modal & Confirmation Hooks at App Root
bytes.body: Lifting UI state like toasts, modals, and confirmation dialogs to app root eliminates prop drilling and keeps components clean. In Vue 3, the idiomatic approach combines `provide`/`inject` with composables. Define a `useToast()` composable backed by a reactive array provided at `App.vue` root — any component injects and calls `toast.add()` without imports beyond the composable. Modals follow the same pattern: a `useModal()` composable exposes `open(component, props)` and renders via `<component :is>` in a root `ModalHost.vue`. Confirmation dialogs are best implemented as promise-based hooks — `useConfirm().ask('Delete item?')` returns a `Promise<boolean>`, letting callers use `await` naturally inside async handlers. The key Vue detail: use `shallowRef` for the modal component to avoid deep reactivity overhead, and `markRaw()` the component reference before storing it. Providing these composables via `app.provide()` in `main.ts` (rather than inside `App.vue`) makes them available before the component tree mounts, which matters for router guards that trigger toasts.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — tree-shaking, lazy-load heavy components, analyze bundle
bytes.title: Vue Bundle Optimization — Tree-Shaking, Lazy Routes, and Analyzing What You Ship
bytes.body:
Vue 3 + Vite gives you tree-shaking out of the box, but you can still bloat your bundle with careless imports. The most common offender: importing all of a UI library instead of individual components. With libraries like Vuestic or PrimeVue, always use their dedicated Vite/rollup plugins for auto-imported tree-shaking rather than a monolithic `app.use(PrimeVue)` with everything registered globally.

For heavy components — rich text editors, chart libraries, PDF viewers — use Vue's `defineAsyncComponent` to defer loading until the component actually mounts. Pair this with Vue Router's lazy route syntax (`component: () => import('./views/Dashboard.vue')`) so Vite emits separate chunks per route automatically.

To audit what you're actually shipping, run `npx vite-bundle-visualizer` (or `rollup-plugin-visualizer` in `vite.config.ts`). It renders an interactive treemap of your dist. Target any single chunk over 200kb — either split it with `build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks` or lazy-load the offending import.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.title: Vue Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, and tsc --noEmit That Actually Catch Bugs
bytes.body:
Vue 3 projects using Vite and TypeScript ship with `vue-tsc` — not plain `tsc` — because template type-checking requires the Vue language plugin. Running `vue-tsc --noEmit` in CI catches broken prop types and missing emits that your editor might silently ignore. Wire it into GitHub Actions as a separate step so it fails fast before tests run.

For pre-commit hygiene, `lint-staged` should scope Prettier to `.vue`, `.ts`, and `.js` files — but pass `--parser babel` only for `.js`; Vue SFCs need `--parser vue` or they'll strip `<template>` formatting. Husky v9 uses the new `husky init` flow: no `.huskyrc`, just shell files in `.husky/`.

Critical gotcha: `eslint-plugin-vue` rules like `vue/no-undef-components` won't fire unless `parserOptions.parser` is set to `@typescript-eslint/parser` inside your flat config — skipping this means component registration errors pass lint silently.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .husky/pre-commit
npx lint-staged

# lint-staged.config.mjs
export default {
  '*.{ts,tsx}': ['eslint --fix', 'prettier --write'],
  '*.vue': ['eslint --fix', 'prettier --parser vue --write'],
  '*.js': ['prettier --parser babel --write'],
};

# .github/workflows/ci.yml (relevant step)
- name: Type-check Vue templates
  run: npx vue-tsc --noEmit

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — Sentry for errors, Web Vitals reporting, source maps
bytes.title: Vue Production Monitoring — Sentry, Web Vitals, and Source Maps Done Right
bytes.body:
Sentry integrates with Vue via its dedicated plugin. Install `@sentry/vue` and initialize it in `main.ts` before mounting your app — pass the `app` instance so Sentry can hook into Vue's global error handler and capture component-level errors with full context, including the failing component name and props trace.

Source maps are critical: without them, Sentry shows minified stack traces. Use `@sentry/vite-plugin` in `vite.config.ts` to automatically upload source maps on build. Set `sourcemap: 'hidden'` so maps are uploaded but never served publicly.

For Web Vitals, pair Sentry's performance tracing with the `web-vitals` package. Report LCP, CLS, and INP directly to Sentry as custom measurements, or use `useRouter()` from Vue Router to instrument navigation spans — Sentry's Vue integration automatically creates transactions per route change when `routingInstrumentation` is configured.

Filter noise early: use `beforeSend` to drop errors from browser extensions and set `tracesSampleRate` to 0.1–0.2 in production to avoid quota exhaustion.

bytes.topic: Reactivity Models — how React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid each decide what re-runs on state change
bytes.title: Vue's Reactivity Model — How the Proxy-Based System Decides What Re-Runs
bytes.body:
Vue 3's reactivity is built on ES Proxy objects. When you call `reactive()` or `ref()`, Vue wraps your data in a Proxy that intercepts `get` and `set` operations. During a component's render (or inside `watchEffect`), Vue's active effect tracker records every reactive property accessed — this is dependency tracking. When a tracked property mutates, only the effects that accessed it are re-queued.

Unlike React's top-down diffing (re-render subtree, then reconcile), Vue tracks at the property level. `ref<number>(0)` exposes a `.value` getter — accessing it inside `<template>` or a `computed()` registers the dependency. Mutating `.value` surgically re-runs only affected computed properties and component renders.

`computed()` is lazy and cached: it re-evaluates only when its own tracked dependencies change, not on every parent render. This means deeply nested components reading stable computed values pay zero re-render cost — no `memo()` equivalent needed. Avoid destructuring reactive objects (`const { count } = state`) — it breaks the Proxy chain and silently kills reactivity.
